name: Guinea

colors.symbolism: the blood of the martyrs and the sacrifice of those who died for liberation; the work of labour
the sun, the source of energy and life; the country's gold and other mineral wealth; justice
Guinea's vegetation and agriculture; solidarity and the prosperity of the people

description: Three equal vertical stripes — red at the hoist, yellow in the centre and green at the fly. The colours echo the Pan-African palette of Ghana and Ethiopia in a vertical arrangement modelled on the French tricolour.
history: Adopted on 10 November 1958, shortly after Guinea's independence from France on 2 October 1958 — the only French West African colony to vote 'No' in the September 1958 referendum on the French Community. Designed under President Sékou Touré, the flag uses the Pan-African red-yellow-green palette of Ghana arranged vertically in homage to the French tricolour. The colours were also those of the Democratic Party of Guinea (PDG), the country's sole legal party at the time.

features.symbolism: the Pan-African colours, here arranged vertically as a deliberate mirror of the French tricolour from which the country gained independence; red for sacrifice, yellow for the sun and Guinea's mineral wealth, green for the land and prosperity
